study.journal: Villar J, Ferrando C, Martínez D, et al. Dexamethasone treatment for the acute respiratory distress syndrome: a multicentre, randomised controlled trial. Lancet Respir Med. 2020;8:267-276.

population_context.population_description: Mechanically ventilated patients aged 18 years or older with established moderate-to-severe ARDS (PaO2/FiO2 ≤200 mm Hg on PEEP ≥10 cm H2O and FiO2 ≥0.5 at 24 hours after ARDS onset) enrolled across 17 ICUs in teaching hospitals in Spain.
population_context.risk_group: Established moderate-to-severe ARDS requiring mechanical ventilation
population_context.exclusions: Brain death, terminal-stage cancer or other disease, do-not-resuscitate status, treatment with corticosteroids or immunosuppressive drugs, enrollment in another experimental treatment protocol, severe chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, congestive heart failure, pregnancy or active lactation.

intervention.name: Intravenous dexamethasone
components: Intravenous dexamethasone 20 mg once daily from day 1 to day 5
Intravenous dexamethasone 10 mg once daily from day 6 to day 10
intervention.parameter_values: 20 mg daily for 5 days, then 10 mg daily for 5 days (maximum 10 days or until extubation if occurring before day 10)
intervention.timing: Started at 24 hours after ARDS onset

comparator.description: Continued routine intensive care without dexamethasone, with lung-protective mechanical ventilation

outcome_effect.outcome_name: Number of ventilator-free days at 28 days (days alive and free from mechanical ventilation from day of randomisation to day 28)

strengths: Multicentre randomised controlled trial with allocation concealment maintained at all sites
Dual primary outcomes including both ventilator-free days and 60-day mortality
Standardized lung-protective ventilation used in both groups
limitations: Trial stopped early due to low enrolment rate before reaching full planned sample size
All sites located in Spain, potentially limiting generalizability
Study enrolled patients with moderate-to-severe ARDS only; findings may not apply to mild ARDS
No stratification by ARDS etiology reported
adverse_events: Hyperglycaemia in the ICU (105 [76%] in dexamethasone group vs 97 [70%] in control group)
New infections in the ICU (eg, pneumonia or sepsis; 33 [24%] vs 35 [25%])
No significant difference in overall adverse event rates between groups

applicability.generalizability_notes: Findings apply to mechanically ventilated patients with established moderate-to-severe ARDS within 24 hours of onset receiving lung-protective ventilation in ICU settings. Applicability to mild ARDS, pediatric populations, or non-mechanically ventilated patients is uncertain. Results are from Spanish teaching hospitals and may have limited generalizability to other healthcare settings.

quoted_sentences.design: This trial was an investigator-initiated, multicentre, randomised controlled trial done in a network of 17 intensive care units (ICUs) in teaching hospitals across Spain.
quoted_sentences.intervention: Patients in the dexamethasone group received an intravenous dose of 20 mg once daily from day 1 to day 5, which was reduced to 10 mg once daily from day 6 to day 10.
quoted_sentences.results: The mean number of ventilator-free days was higher in the dexamethasone group than in the control group (between-group difference 4·8 days [95% CI 2·57 to 7·03]; p<0·0001). At 60 days, 29 (21%) patients in the dexamethasone group and 50 (36%) patients in the control group had died (between-group difference –15·3% [–25·9 to –4·9]; p=0·0047).
quoted_sentences.outcome: Early administration of dexamethasone could reduce duration of mechanical ventilation and overall mortality in patients with established moderate-to-severe ARDS.
